key_points: Atoms are the fundamental units of matter and retain their identity during chemical reactions
Molecules are formed by the chemical combination of two or more atoms in definite proportions
Elements are pure substances made of only one kind of atom; compounds are made of two or more elements in fixed ratios
Law of multiple proportions: When two elements form more than one compound, the ratio of masses of one element that combines with fixed mass of the other is a small whole number
Dalton’s atomic theory explains the nature of atoms and formation of molecules
Molecular mass is the sum of atomic masses of all atoms in a molecule
Formula unit is the smallest repeating unit of an ionic compound (e.g., NaCl)
Empirical formula gives the simplest whole number ratio of elements in a compound
examples: CO and CO₂ follow the law of multiple proportions: 12g C combines with 16g O to form CO and with 32g O to form CO₂
H₂O has molecular mass 18 u (2×1 + 16), NH₃ has molecular mass 17 u (14+3×1)
NaCl’s formula unit has 1 Na⁺ and 1 Cl⁻
Empirical formula of glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) is CH₂O
Hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) and water (H₂O) illustrate molecules with same elements in different proportions
formulas: Molecular mass = Sum of atomic masses of all atoms in molecule
Mass percent of element = (Mass of element / Molecular mass) × 100
Empirical formula ratio = Divide atomic ratios by greatest common factor
Mass ratio in compounds = Mass of element1 / Mass of element2 according to formula
exam_tips: Use Dalton’s atomic theory to explain formation of molecules and compounds
Apply law of multiple proportions in numerical problems
Distinguish between molecular formula, empirical formula, and formula unit
Calculate molecular mass, mass percent, and mass ratios in compounds
Provide clear examples of elements, molecules, and compounds in exams
